THE
INDIA
OFFICE LIST

FOR

1907

A rectangular stamp with a curved top edge. The text "INDIA OFFICE" is arched along the top, and "LIBRARY" is centered below it in a straight line.

COMPILED FROM OFFICIAL RECORDS BY DIRECTION OF THE SECRETARY
OF STATE FOR INDIA IN COUNCIL.

LONDON:
HARRISON AND SONS, 45, PALL MALL,
BOOKSELLERS TO HIS MAJESTY AND H.R.H. THE PRINCE OF WALES.

1907.

Price Ten Shillings and Sixpence.

P R E F A C E.

In the lists of Officers serving in India, which are contained in pages 7 to 121 of this volume, only substantive appointments are shown; and, except in the case of members of the Indian Civil Service, the names of Officers drawing substantive pay of less than Rs. 500 a month are not included. The names shown in italics are those of Officers who are in foreign service, supernumerary, seconded, or employed outside their own departments.

The principle of selection followed in the Record of Services (pages 425 to 672) is explained on page 423.

To meet the difficulty that arises from the lack of uniformity in India in the use of surnames, Indian names are twice indexed, and cross references are made in the Record of Services. Rulers of States are also shown under the names of their States.

An Alphabetical List of the principal Native Chiefs of India, showing their titles (abbreviated), has been included in this Edition for the first time, and will be found on pages 197-199.

The "Index of Subjects and Appointments" is on pages iii to xiv, and the "Index of Names" on pages 681 to 723. Names in the Honours Lists and Record of Services are shown in the Index of Names by "B" and "R," with a foot-note referring to the Alphabetical Lists on pages 171 to 189 and pages 425 to 672.

A Casualty List (Retirements and Deaths) is given on pages 673 to 679.

It is requested that intimations of errors in the lists, or of omissions, may be addressed to the Editor of the India List, India Office, London, S.W.

THE INDIA OFFICE LIST.

OFFICE OF
THE SECRETARY OF STATE FOR INDIA
IN COUNCIL,
ST. JAMES'S PARK, S.W.

SECRETARY OF STATE.
THE RIGHT HON. JOHN MORLEY, O.M., M.P.

UNDER SECRETARIES OF STATE.

Sir Arthur Godley, K.C.B. | C. E. H. Hobhouse, M.P.

ASSISTANT UNDER SECRETARY OF STATE.

Sir Horace G. Walpole, K.C.B., J.P.

COUNCIL.

Vice-President.—Sir William Lee-Warner, K.C.S.I.

Sir Dennis Fitzpatrick, K.C.S.I.Sir Hugh S. Barnes, K.C.S.I., K.C.V.O.
Sir James L. Mackay, G.C.M.G., K.C.I.E.Lieut.-Col. Sir David W. K. Barr, K.C.S.I.
Sir John Edge, K.C.Sir Felix O. Schuster, Bart.
Sir Philip P. Hutchins, K.C.S.I.Theodore Morison.
Gen. Sir Alexander R. Badcock, K.C.B., C.S.I.Gen. Sir Charles C. Egerton, G.C.B., D.S.O.

Clerk of the Council.—Sir Horace G. Walpole, K.C.B., J.P.

Private Secretary to the Secretary of State.—F. A. Hirtzel.

Assistant Private Secretary and Precis Writer.—P. H. Dumbell.

Asst. Private Secretary (unpaid).—Hon. O. Brett.

Political A.D.C. to the Secretary of State.—Lieut.-Colonel Sir W. H. Curzon Wyllie,
K.C.I.E., M.V.O.

INDIAN TROOP SERVICE.

The business of the Troop Service is under the superintendence of Vice-Admiral Sir G. T. H. Boyes, K.C.B., R.N., Director of Transports at the Admiralty.

INDIA OFFICE LIBRARY.

The Library was founded in 1801 by the East India Company. It contains about 70,000 printed books, principally relating to the East, and 15,000 Oriental manuscripts and xylographs.

There are the following catalogues, and others are in preparation :—

ORIENTAL MANUSCRIPTS AND XYLOGRAPHs.IN PRINT.

Sanskrit.—Mackenzie Collection. H. H. Wilson. Calcutta, 1828, 2 vols.
" General Collection. J. Eggeling. London, 1888, &c.
" Burnell Collection (Vedic MSS.). A. C. Burnell. London, 1869.
" Hodgson Collection. Sir W. W. Hunter. Life of B. H. Hodgson, p. 344. London, 1896.
" Tagore Collection. S. Mohun Tagore. Calcutta, 1890.
" " " T. Aufrecht. Zeitsch. der Deutsch. Morgenländ. Gesellschaft, lviii, pp. 525-38, 1904.
" Böhler Collection. Zeitsch. der Deutsch. Morgenländ. Gesellschaft, xlii, pp. 530-52, 1888.
" Royal Society Collection. C. H. Tawney and F. W. Thomas. London, 1903.
Arabic.—O. Loth. London, 1877.
Arabic and Persian.—E. Denison Ross and E. G. Browne. London, 1902.
Persian.—H. Ethé. Vol. I. Oxford, 1903.
Chinese.—Rev. J. Summers. London, 1872.
" (Tripitaka). S. Beal. Devonport, 1876.
" " Bunyiu Nanjio. Oxford, 1883.
Malay.—H. van der Tuuk. Tijdschr. voor Nederlandsch Ind., 1849 (1), pp. 385-400.
Pali.—H. Oldenberg. (Journ. Pali Text Soc., London, 1882, pp. 59-128.)
" (Mandalay), V. Fausbøll. (Journ. Pali Text Soc., Woking, 1897, pp. 1-52.)

IN MANUSCRIPT.

Sanskrit.—Mackenzie Collections (R. Rost : incomplete).
Hindustani.—Delhi Collection.
Arabic.—Delhi Collection.
Persian.—Delhi Collection.
Ethiopic and Syriac.—By W. Wright.

Chinese.—By S. Beal.
" Royal Society Collection. By H. A. Giles.
Burmese.—By E. Chevilliot.
" (Mandalay), by Taw Sein Ko (Notes).
Siamese.—By H. Alabaster.
Batta.—By H. Van der Tuuk.

PRINTED BOOKS AND LITHOGRAPHS.

Catalogue, London, 1845, and Supplement, 1851 (superseded).
" Vol. I and Index. London, 1888.
" Supplement. London, 1895.
" Vol. II, Pt. 1. Sanskrit Books. R. Rost. London, 1897.
" Pt. 2. Hindustani Books. J. F. Blumhardt. London, 1900.
" Pt. 3. Hindi, Panjabi, Pushtu, and Sindhi Books. J. F. Blumhardt. Lond., 1902.
" Pt. 4. Bengali, Oriya, and Assamese Books. J. F. Blumhardt. London, 1905.
" Pt. 5. Marathi and Gujarati Books. J. F. Blumhardt. London, 1907.

The following are the Rules as to the use of Books and Manuscripts :—

  1. 1. Persons qualified to borrow books and MSS. from the Library are members of both Services, retired members, and such individuals as are either well known to the Librarian or produce a suitable introduction; in all cases books can only be borrowed with the sanction of the Librarian, who shall take from each person an acknowledgment for whatever he may be permitted to take away on loan. In the case of an Oriental manuscript, the approval of the authorities must first be obtained; and, except in special cases, a bond for the safe return of the manuscript will be required.
  2. 2. No book thus borrowed shall be kept more than three months; but when returned, it may be borrowed again by the same reader, unless wanted by another reader, or required in the Library for any purpose. In the case, however, of scholars engaged in special investigations, it shall be in the power of the Librarian to extend the loan of any book without insisting on its being previously returned, provided that the borrower has certified in writing that the book is in good condition. No periodical shall be kept longer than one week. A work may be recalled at any time, at the discretion of the Librarian.
  3. 3. Other persons who wish merely to refer to the books in the Library may do so upon entering their names and addresses in a book kept for that purpose, and signing a requisition for the same on a printed form. The reader will be responsible for the work thus signed for until this form shall have been returned to him.

The Library is open from 10 a.m. to 5 p.m., on Saturdays 10 a.m. to 2 p.m.; closed during the month of September and on holidays.

PROBATIONERS FOR THE INDIAN FOREST SERVICE.

These Probationers undergo a special course of study for two years at Oxford, and receive in their third year practical instruction on the Continent. Their training is under the superintendence of the Principal Professor of Forestry, Dr. W. Schlich, C.I.E., Ph.D., F.R.S., F.L.S., formerly Inspector-General of Forests to the Government of India, and the Assistant Professor of Forestry, Mr. W. R. Fisher, B.A., formerly Conservator of Forests, Indian Forest Service.

Arrangements are also occasionally made for the training of Probationers for service elsewhere than in India.

The following is a list of the Probationers now being trained by the Secretary of State, all of them being Probationers for the Indian Forest Service, except where otherwise stated :—

THIRD YEAR.

Barrington, A. H. M. Homfray, J. Rowbotham, C. J.
Cox, C. E. C. Mead, J. P. Sanger-Davies, A. E.
Forbes, V. H. Osmaston, A. E. Whitehead, T. A.
Grieve, A. N. Powell, W. S. Wimbush, A.
Hewett, D. P.

It is proposed to allot Mr. Mead and Mr. Sanger-Davies to the Malay States Forest Service.

SECOND YEAR.

Clarence, G. C. Glover, H. M. Milroy, A. J. W.
Clear, T. Gunn, J. Minchin, A. A. F.
Dawkins, C. G. E. Hepburn, J. K. Robinson, R. L.
Gaunt, C. C. Jolly, N. W. Smythies, E. A.
Gibson, H. S. Miller, W. A. H. Wilson, C. C.

It is proposed to allot one of the foregoing to the Sudan Forest Service.

FIRST YEAR.

Benskin, E. Holland, L. B. Reed, R. W., B.A.
Bond, H. M. Kilmartin, A. J. Robinson, G.
Butterworth, G. S. Lawton, W. Sargent, J. D.
Carr, J. Milner, C. E. Shirley, G. S.
Davis, A. P. Oliphant, F. M. Sitzler, E. A.
Felton, A. L., B.A. Oliphant, J. N. Tear, F. J.
Fields-Clarke, V. H. T. Owden, J. S. Wright, H. L.
Herbert, V. A., B.A. Rambaut, A. E. Young, J. V.

It is proposed to allot two of the foregoing to the Ceylon Forest Service, one to the Malay States Forest Service, and two to the Sudan Forest Service.

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA.

VICEROY AND GOVERNOR-GENERAL OF INDIA.

His Excellency the Right Hon. the EARL OF MINTO, G.C.M.G., G.M.S.I., G.M.I.E., assumed charge of office, 18 November, 1905.

PERSONAL STAFF OF THE GOVERNOR-GENERAL.

Private Secretary, Lieut.-Col. J. R. Dunlop-Smith, C.I.E. Native Aides-de-Camp, Abdul Aziz, Risaldar Major, Sardar Bahadur, 5th Cavy; Madho Singh Rana, Subadar Major, Sardar Bahadur, 2nd Bn. 4th Gurkha Rifles.
Military Secretary, Major F. L. Adam, M.V.O., 1st Bn. Scots Guards. Surgeon, Surgn.-Lieut.-Col. W. R. Crooke-Lawless, M.D., Coldstream Gds.
Comptroller of the Household, Capt. T. H. R. Bulkeley, 1st Bn. Scots Gds. (Offic.). Commandant of Body Guard, Capt. H. N. Holden, 5th Cavy.
Aides-de-Camp, Major G. P. T. Feilding, D.S.O., 1st Bn. Coldstream Gds.; Lieut. Lord Francis G. M. Douglas Scott, 2nd Bn. Grenadier Gds. Adjutant of Body Guard, Capt. V. A. S. Keighley, 18th P.W.O. Tiwana Lncrs.

CIVIL ANNUITANTS, BENGAL ESTABLISHMENT.
Appd. Ann. Name. Appd. Ann. Name.
1861 1896 Adams, G. 1852 1879 Chapman, C. E.

POSTAL ACCOUNT OFFICERS.

Comptroller—A. H. Anthony.

Deputy Comptrollers, 1st Class—J. A. O'Brien. 2nd Class—E. H. Burke, W. A. St. L. Kelly.

POST OFFICE.

Director-General of the Post Office of India—C. Stewart-Wilson.
Deputy Director-General—C. H. Harrison.
Postmasters-General, 1st Grade—W. Maxwell, E. A. Doran, C.I.E., C. H. Harrison.
2nd Grade—Cowasji Jamshedji Lalkaka, Khan Bahadur, G. R. Clarke, W. F. Cockell, H. N. Hutchinson.
Inspector-General, Railway Mail Service—E. C. O'Brien.
Deputy Inspector-General, Railway Mail Service—Vacant.

SURVEY DEPARTMENT.

The names of the officers in Survey Department serving in the Punjab will be found under "India." (See page 33.)

POLITICAL DEPARTMENT.AGENTS TO THE LIEUTENANT-GOVERNOR.

The Commissioner of the Delhi Division for the time being, for Pataudi, Dujana, Loharu, Kalsia, Sirmur (Nahan).
Do. of the Jullundur Division for the time being, for Kapurthala, Mandi, Suket, Faridkot and Maler Kotla.
Do. of the Lahore Division for the time being, for Chamba.
Do. of the Multan Division for the time being for Frontier Tribes bordering on the districts of his Division.

POLITICAL DEPARTMENT—continued.AGENTS TO THE LIEUTENANT-GOVERNOR—continued.

The Superintendent, Hill States, Simla, for the time being, for the Hill States.
The Political Agent, Phulkian States and Bahawalpur, for the time being, for the Patiala, Bahawalpur, Jind, and Nabha States.

HILL STATES.

Comprising Bilaspur (Kahlur), Bashahr, Nalagarh (Hindur), Keonthal, Baghal, Baghat, Jubbal, Kumharsain, Bhajji, Mailog, Balsan, Dhami, Kuthar, Kunihar, Mangal, Bija, Darkuti, Taroch, Sangri, Kaneti, Dalti, Koti, Theog, Madhan, Ghond, Ratesh, Rawin, and Dhadi. The Deputy Commissioner of Simla is ex officio Superintendent.

FRONTIER TRIBES.

The Deputy Commissioner of Dera Ghazi Khan is Political Officer with respect to the Independent Tribes bordering on his district.

PHULKIAN STATES AND BAHAWALPUR.

Political Agent—Major W. M. Dallas, I.A.

Personal Assistant—

PATIALA STATE.

Settlement Commissioner—Major F. Popham Young, C.I.E., I.A.

Medical Adviser—Major C. H. James, I.M.S.

English Tutor and Guardian to H.H. the Maharaja—A. C. Sells, M.A.

BOMBAY.

Governor—HIS EXCELLENCY THE RIGHT HON. SIR CHARLES WALLACE ALEXANDER NAPIER, BARON LAMINGTON, G.C.M.G., G.C.I.E.

Took his seat 12th December, 1903.

COUNCIL.

E. McG. H. Fulton, C.S.I., took his seat 24th April, 1902.

J. W. P. Muir-Mackenzie, C.S.I., took his seat 6th August, 1905.

JUDICIAL DEPARTMENT.HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE.

(Established 1862.)

Chief Justice—Sir Lawrence Hugh Jenkins, K.C.I.E.Puisne Judges—L. P. Russell, Narayan Ganesh Chandavarkar, H. Batty, S. L. Batchelor,

Maj. T. D. C. Barry ... Professor of Chemistry and of Medical Jurisprudence, Grant Medical College; Chemical Analyser to Government.
Maj. A. Street, M.B. ... Second Surgeon Jamsetjee Jijibhoy Hospital; Presidency Surgeon, First District; Professor of Anatomy and Curator of Museum, Grant Medical College.
Lieut.-Col. B. B. Grayfoot ... Presidency Surgeon, Second District and Marine Surgeon; Supdt., Lunatic Asylum, Colaba; in Medical Charge, Elphinstone College.
Lieut.-Col. J. P. Barry, M.B. ... Presidency Surgeon, Third District. In Medical charge of Common Prison, House of Correction, and Byculla Schools.
Lieut.-Col. M. A. T. Collie, M.B. Physician to St. George's Hospital, Bombay.
Capt. A. F. W. King ... Resident Surgeon, St. George's Hospital, Professor of Materia Medica and Pharmacy, Grant Medical College.
V.C. Lt.-Col. J. Crimmin, C.I.E. Health Officer of the Port of Bombay.
Capt. H. H. Kiddle... .. Probationer in Chemical Examiner's Department.

CHRONOLOGICAL LISTS OF HEADS OF ADMINISTRATION
IN INDIA AND AT HOME.

CHAIRMEN AND DEPUTY-CHAIRMEN OF THE EAST INDIA COMPANY.
(SINCE THE REGULATING ACT OF 1773.)

Year. Chairman. Deputy-Chairman.
1773 Henry Crabbe Boulton (a). Edward Wheeler.
1774 Edward Wheeler. John Harrison.
1775 John Harrison. John Roberts.
1776 John Roberts. William James.
1777 George Wombwell. William Devaynes.
1778 George Wombwell (b) (2nd time). William James (f) (2nd time).
1779 Sir William James, Bart. William Devaynes (2nd time).
1780 William Devaynes. Laurence Sullivan.
1781 Laurence Sullivan. Sir William James, Bart. (3rd time).
1782 Robert Gregory. Henry Fletcher (g).
1783 Sir Henry Fletcher, Bart. (c). Nathaniel Smith.
1784 Nathaniel Smith. William Devaynes (3rd time).
1785 William Devaynes (2nd time). Nathaniel Smith (2nd time).
1786 John Michie. John Motteux.
1787 John Motteux. Nathaniel Smith (3rd time).
1788 Nathaniel Smith (2nd time). John Michie (h).
1789 William Devaynes (3rd time). Stephen Lushington.
1790 Stephen Lushington (d). William Devaynes (4th time).
1791 John Smith Burges. Francis Baring.
1792 Francis Baring. John Smith Burges.
1793 William Devaynes (4th time). Thomas Cheap.
1794 William Devaynes (5th time). John Hunter.
1795 Sir Stephen Lushington, Bart. (2nd time). David Scott.
1796 David Scott. Hugh Inglis.
1797 Hugh Inglis. Jacob Bosanquet.
1798 Jacob Bosanquet. Sir Stephen Lushington, Bart. (2nd time).
1799 Sir Stephen Lushington, Bart. (3rd time). Hugh Inglis (2nd time).
1800 Hugh Inglis (2nd time). David Scott (2nd time).
1801 David Scott (e) (2nd time). Charles Mills.
1802 John Roberts (2nd time). Jacob Bosanquet (2nd time).
1803 Jacob Bosanquet (2nd time). John Roberts (2nd time).
1804 Hon. W. Fullarton Elphinstone. Charles Grant.
1805 Charles Grant. George Smith.
1806 Hon. W. Fullarton Elphinstone (2nd time). Edward Parry.
1807 Edward Parry. Charles Grant (2nd time).
1808 Edward Parry (2nd time). Charles Grant (3rd time).
1809 Charles Grant (2nd time). William Astell.
1810 William Astell. Jacob Bosanquet (3rd time).
1811 Jacob Bosanquet (3rd time). Sir Hugh Inglis, Bart. (3rd time).
1812 Sir Hugh Inglis, Bart. (3rd time). Robert Thornton.
1813 Robert Thornton. Hon. W. Fullarton Elphinstone.
1814 Hon. W. Fullarton Elphinstone (3rd time). John Inglis.
1815 Charles Grant (3rd time). Thomas Reid.
1816 Thomas Reid. John Bebb.

(a.) On the death of Mr. Boulton the Court, on October 12, appointed Edward Wheeler, Chairman, and John Harrison, Deputy.

(b.) Afterwards Sir George Wombwell, Bart.

(c.) Sir Henry Fletcher being disqualified, the Court, on November 26, appointed Nathaniel Smith, Chairman, and on the next day, William Devaynes, Deputy.

(d.) Afterwards Sir Stephen Lushington, Bart.

(e.) Mr. Scott resigned the Chair, and, on September 2, the Court appointed Chas. Mills, Chairman, and John Roberts, Deputy.

(f.) Afterwards Sir William James, Bart.

(g.) Afterwards Sir Henry Fletcher, Bart. Mr. Gregory being disqualified, the Court, on July 31, appointed Sir Henry Fletcher, Chairman, and, the next day, Nathaniel Smith, Deputy.

(h.) William Devaynes appointed, 2nd December, Deputy Chairman, in place of John Michie, deceased

Year of Appointment. Name. Year of Appointment. Name. Year of Appointment. Name.
1600 R. Wright. 1666 R. Blackborne. 1792 W. Ramsay.
1607 W. Leighton. 1701 T. Woolley. 1814 J. Cobb.
1613 F. Sadler. 1728 T. Michell. 1818 J. Dart.
1620 R. Bacon. 1731 C. Mole. 1820 P. Auber
1625 E. Sherburne. 1748 R. James. 1836 J. C. Melvill(a).
1641 R. Swinglehurst. 1768 P. Michell. 1858 J. D. Dickinson.
1654 J. Stanyan. 1783 T. Morton.

NOTE.—The dates against the first three names on the above list indicate years in which those gentlemen are known to have held the post of Secretary. The exact date of their respective appointments is uncertain.

(a) Afterwards Sir James C. Melvill, K.C.B.

(l). " (by creation) Baron Lyveden.

(m). " (by succession) Earl of Derby.

GOVERNORS-GENERAL OF FORT WILLIAM IN BENGAL.
Name. Assumed charge of office. Remarks.
Warren Hastings ... .. 20 Oct., 1774 Appointed Governor of Bengal, 13 April, 1772, and first Governor-General under an Act passed in 1773 (13 Geo. III, cap. 63).
Sir John Macpherson, Bart. ... .. 8 Feb., 1785
Earl Cornwallis, K.G. (a) ... .. 12 Sept., 1786 Also Commander-in-Chief.
Sir John Shore, Bart. (b) ... .. 28 Oct., 1793
Lieut.-General the Hon. Sir Alured Clarke, K.C.B. 17 March, 1798 Officiating. Senior Member of Council; also Commander-in-Chief.
The Earl of Mornington, P.C. (c) ... 18 May, 1798
The Marquis Cornwallis, K.G. (2nd time) 30 July, 1805 Also Commander-in-Chief; died at Ghazipur, 5 October, 1805.
Sir George H. Barlow, Bart. ... .. 10 Oct., 1805 Confirmed, 25 Feb., 1806.
Lord Minto, P.C. (d) ... .. 31 July, 1807
The Earl of Moira, K.G., P.C. (e) 4 Oct., 1813 Also Commander-in-Chief.
John Adam ... .. 13 Jan., 1823 Officiating. Senior Member of Council.
Lord Amherst, P.C. (f) ... .. 1 Aug., 1823
William Butterworth Bayley ... .. 13 March, 1828 Officiating. Senior Member of Council.
Lord William Cavendish Bentinck, G.C.B., G.C.H., P.C. 4 July, 1828 Also Commander-in-Chief from 16 May, 1833.

(a) Created Marquess Cornwallis, 15 Aug., 1792.

(b) Afterwards (by creation) Baron Teignmouth.

(c) Created Marquess Wellesley, 2 Dec., 1799.

(d) Created Earl of Minto, 24 Feb., 1813.

(e) Created Marquess of Hastings, 2 Dec., 1816.

(f) Created Earl Amherst, 2 Dec., 1826.

GOVERNORS-GENERAL OF INDIA.
Name. Assumed charge of office. Remarks.
Lord William Cavendish Bentinck, G.C.B., G.C.H., P.C. 14 Nov., 1834 Also Commander-in-Chief.
Sir Charles Metcalfe, Bart. (a) ... 20 March, 1835 Officiating upon nomination of the Directors, which was disallowed by His Majesty's Govt.
Lord Auckland, G.C.B., P.C. (b) ... 4 March, 1836
Lord Ellenborough, P.C. (c) ... .. 28 Feb., 1842
William Wilberforce Bird ... .. 15 June, 1844 Officiating. Senior member of council.
The Right Hon. Sir Henry Hardinge, G.C.B. (d) 23 July, 1844
The Earl of Dalhousie, P.C. (e) ... 12 Jan., 1848
Viscount Canning, P.C. (f) ... .. 29 Feb., 1856

(a) Afterwards (by creation) Baron Metcalfe.

(b) Created Earl of Auckland, 21 Dec., 1839.

(c) Afterwards (by creation) Earl of Ellenborough.

(d) Created Viscount Hardinge, 2 May, 1846.

(e) Created Marquess of Dalhousie, 25 Aug., 1849.

(f) Afterwards (by creation) Earl Canning.

VICEROYS AND GOVERNORS-GENERAL OF INDIA.
Name. Assumed charge of office. Remarks.
Viscount Canning, P.C. (a) ... .. 1 Nov., 1858 First G.M.S.I. (1861).
The Earl of Elgin and Kincardine, K.T., G.C.B., P.C. 12 March, 1862 Died at Dharmasala, 20 Nov., 1863.
Major-General Sir Robert Napier, K.C.B. (b) 21 Nov., 1863 Officiating, pending the arrival of the Governor of Madras. Senior Member of Council.
Colonel Sir William T. Denison, K.C.B. 2 Dec., 1863 Officiating under the Act of 1861. Governor of Madras.

(a) Created Earl Canning, 21 May, 1859.

(b) Afterwards (by creation) Baron Napier (of Magdala)

VICEROYS AND GOVERNORS-GENERAL OF INDIA—continued.
Name. Assumed charge of office. Remarks.
The Right Hon. Sir John Lawrence,
Bart., G.C.B., K.C.S.I. (a)		 12 Jan., 1864
The Earl of Mayo, K.P.... .. 12 Jan., 1869 Assassinated at Port Blair, 8 Feb., 1872.
John Strachey (b) ... .. 9 Feb., 1872 Officiating, pending the arrival of the Governor of Madras. Senior Member of Council.
Lord Napier of Merchistown, K.T. (c) 23 Feb., 1872 Governor of Madras, Officiating.
Lord Northbrook, P.C. (d) ... .. 3 May, 1872
Lord Lytton, G.C.B. (e) ... .. 12 April, 1876
The Marquess of Ripon, K.G., P.C.... 8 June, 1880
The Earl of Dufferin, K.P., G.C.B.
G.C.M.G., P.C. (f)		 13 Dec., 1884 First G.M.I.E. (1887).
The Marquess of Lansdowne, G.C.M.G. 10 Dec., 1888
The Earl of Elgin and Kincardine, P.C. 27 Jan., 1894
Lord Curzon of Kedleston, P.C. ... 6 Jan., 1899
Lord Amphill ... .. 30 April, 1904 Governor of Madras, Officiating.
Lord Curzon of Kedleston, P.C. ... 13 Dec., 1904 Reappointed.
The Earl of Minto, P.C., G.C.M.G.,
G.M.S.I., G.M.I.E. (g)		 18 Nov., 1905

(a) Afterwards (by creation) Baron Lawrence.

(b) Afterwards Sir John Strachey, G.C.S.I., C.I.E.

(c) Afterwards (by creation) Baron Napier of Ettrick.

(d) Afterwards (by creation) Earl of Northbrook.

(e) Created Earl of Lytton, 28 April, 1880.

(f) Created Marquis of Dufferin and Ava, 12 Nov. 1888.

(g) During tenure of office, the Viceroy is Grand Master and First and Principal Knight of the two Indian Orders (G.M.S.I. and G.M.I.E.). On quitting office, he becomes G.C.S.I. and G.C.I.E., with the date of his assumption of the Viceroyalty.

COMMANDERS-IN-CHIEF IN INDIA

SINCE THE REGULATING ACT OF 1773.

Name. Assumed charge of office. Remarks.
Lieut.-General John Clavering ... 27 Oct., 1774 Appointed Member of Council of Governor-General in the Act of 1773; took his seat 20 Oct., 1774. Died, 30 August, 1777.
Brig.-General Giles Stibbert ... 16 Oct., 1777 Officiating.
Lieut.-General Sir Eyre Coote, K.B.... 25 Mar., 1779 Member of Council from 24 March, 1779, Died, 26 April, 1783.
Brig.-General Giles Stibbert ... 27 April, 1783 Officiating.
General Robert Sloper ... 21 July, 1785 Member of Council from 21 July, 1785.
Earl Cornwallis, K.G. ... 12 Sept., 1786 Also Governor-General.
Maj.-Gen. Sir Robert Abercromby, K.B. 28 Oct., 1793 Member of Council from 28 Oct., 1793.
Lieut.-General Sir Alured Clarke, K.B. 17 May, 1798 Member of Council from 17 Mar., 1797.
Lieut.-General Gerard Lake (a) ... 14 May, 1801 Member of Council from 14 Mar., 1801, to 26 Feb., 1807.
The Marquis Cornwallis, K.G.... 30 July, 1805 Also Governor-General. Died 5 Oct., 1805.
Lieut.-General Lord Lake ... 10 Oct., 1805 Member of Council from 14 Mar., 1801, to 26 Feb., 1807.
Lieut.-General Sir George Hewitt ... 17 Oct., 1807 Member of Council from 17 Oct, 1807.
Lieut.-Gen. Sir George Nugent, Bart. 14 Jan., 1812 Member of Council from 14 Jan., 1812, to 2 Dec., 1814.
General The Earl of Moira, K.G., P.C.(b) 4 Oct., 1813 Also Governor-General.
Lieut.-General The Hon. Sir Edward
Paget, G.C.B.		 13 Jan., 1823 Member of Council; took his seat, 13 Jan., 1823.
General Lord Combermere, G.C.B.,
G.C.H.		 7 Oct., 1825 Member of Council; took his seat, 7 Oct., 1825.

(a) Afterwards (by creation) Baron Lake (1804), and Viscount Lake (1807).

(b) Created Marquess of Hastings, 2 December, 1816.

COMMANDERS-IN-CHIEF IN INDIA—continued.
Name. Assumed charge of office. Remarks.
Lieut.-General The Earl of Dalhousie, G.C.B. 1 Jan., 1830 Member of Council; took his seat, 1 Jan., 1830.
Lieut.-General Sir Edward Barnes, G.C.B. 10 Jan., 1832 Member of Council; took his seat, 10 Jan., 1832.
General Lord Wm. C. Bentinck, G.C.B. 16 May, 1833 Also Governor-General. Left India, 20 March, 1835.
Lieut.-General Sir Henry Fane, G.C.B. 5 Sept., 1835 Extraordinary Member of Council; took his seat, 5 Sept., 1835.
General Sir Jasper Nicolls, K.C.B. ... 7 Dec., 1839 Extraordinary Member of Council; took his seat, 7 Dec., 1839.
General Sir Hugh Gough, G.C.B. (a) 11 Aug., 1843 Extraordinary Member of Council; took his seat, 11 Aug., 1843.
General Sir Charles J. Napier, G.C.B. 7 May, 1849 Extraordinary Member of Council; took his seat, 7 May, 1849. Resigned, Dec., 1850.
General Sir Wm. M. Gomm, K.C.B. 6 Dec., 1850 Extraordinary Member of Council; took his seat, 6 Dec., 1850.
General The Hon. Geo. Anson ... 23 Jan., 1856 Extraordinary Member of Council; took his seat, 23 Jan., 1856. Died, 27 May, 1857.
Lieut.-General Sir Colin Campbell, G.C.B. (b) 13 Aug., 1857 Extraordinary Member of Council; took his seat, 31 Aug., 1857.
General Sir Hugh H. Rose, G.C.B. (c) 4 June, 1860 Extraordinary Member of Council; took his seat, 6 July, 1860.
General Sir Wm. Rose Mansfield, K.C.B. (d) 23 March, 1865 Extraordinary Member of Council; took his seat, 27 April, 1865.
General Lord Napier of Magdala, G.C.B., G.C.S.I. 9 April, 1870 Extraordinary Member of Council; took his seat, 25 May, 1870.
General Sir Fred P. Haines, K.C.B. ... 10 April, 1876 Extraordinary Member of Council; took his seat, 15 April, 1876.
General Sir Donald M. Stewart, G.C.B., C.I.E. 8 April, 1881 Extraordinary Member of Council; took his seat, 8 April, 1881.
General Sir Fred. S. Roberts, V.C., G.C.B., C.I.E. (e) 28 Nov., 1885 Extraordinary Member of Council; took his seat, 29th Jan., 1886.
General Sir Geo. S. White, V.C., G.C.I.E., K.C.B. 8 April, 1893 Extraordinary Member of Council from 1 April, 1893.
Lieut.-General Sir Charles Edward Nairne, K.C.B. 20 March, 1898 Provisional. Extraordinary Member of Council from 4 April, 1898.
General Sir W. S. A. Lockhart, G.C.B., K.C.S.I. 4 Nov., 1898 Extraordinary Member of Council from 4 Nov., 1898. Died, 18 March, 1900.
General Sir A. P. Palmer, K.C.B. ... 19 March, 1900 Provisional. Confirmed, 26 March, 1901. Extraordinary Member of Council from 22 April, 1901.
General Viscount Kitchener of Khartoum, G.C.B., O.M., G.C.M.G. 28 Nov., 1902 Extraordinary Member of Council; took his seat, 28 Nov. 1902.

(a) Created Baron Gough, 25 April, 1846, and afterwards Viscount Gough.

(b) Created Baron Clyde, 3 July, 1858.

(c) Afterwards (by creation), Baron Strathnairn.

(d) Afterwards (by creation), Baron Sandhurst.

(e) Afterwards (by creation) Earl Roberts.

Resigned, 17 Jan., 1838. First holder of the post of Law Member. He and his successors down to 1853 had no power to sit and vote except at meetings for the purpose of making laws and regulations.

PRESIDENTS AND GOVERNORS OF FORT ST. GEORGE IN MADRAS.
Name. Assumed charge of office. Remarks.
William Gyfford (a) ... .. 16 June, 1684
Elihu Yale (b) ... .. 25 July, 1687
Nathaniel Higginson ... .. 3 Oct., 1692
Thomas Pitt ... .. 7 July, 1698
Gulston Addison ... .. 18 Sept., 1709 Died at Madras, 17 Oct., 1709.
Edmund Montague ... .. 17 Oct., 1709 Acting governor.
William Fraser ... .. 3 Nov., 1709 Acting governor.
Edward Harrison ... .. 11 July, 1711
Joseph Collet ... .. 8 Jan., 1717
Francis Hastings ... .. 18 Jan., 1720 Acting governor.
Nathaniel Elwick ... .. 15 Oct., 1721
James Macrae ... .. 15 Jan., 1725
George Morton Pitt ... .. 14 May, 1730
Richard Benyon ... .. 23 Jan., 1735
Nicholas Morse (c) ... .. 17 Jan., 1744
John Hinde (d) ... .. ...
Charles Floyer (e) ... .. 16 April, 1747
Thomas Saunders (f) ... .. 19 Sept., 1750
George Pigot ... .. 14 Jan., 1755
Robert Palk ... .. 14 Nov., 1763
Charles Bouchier ... .. 25 Jan., 1767
Josias DuPre ... .. 31 Jan., 1770
Alexander Wynch ... .. 2 Feb., 1773

(a) Madras, which had previously been an Agency under the jurisdiction of the Presidency of Bantam in Java, was created a Presidency in 1652, Bantam being in turn placed under Madras, and the President, Mr. Baker, transferred to the latter station. This arrangement, however, continued only to 1655, when the settlement again became an Agency, and so remained till 1684, when the Agent, Mr. Gyfford, was made "President and Governor of all the English East India Company's affairs upon the coast of Choromandel and in the Bay of Bengala."

(b) Mr. Yale also acted during Mr. Gyfford's absence in Bengal from 8th Aug., 1684, to 26th Jan., 1685.

(c) Madras having been captured by the French on the 10th September, 1746, the government of the Settlement devolved on Mr. John Hinde, the Deputy Governor of Fort St. David.

(d) Mr. Hinde died at Fort St. David on the 14th April, 1747, previous to the receipt of the Court of Directors' despatch of 24th Jan., 1747, creating Fort St. David the Head Settlement, and appointing Mr. Hinde president and governor.

(e) The Court's despatch ordering Mr. Floyer's dismissal from the service was received at Fort St. David on the 6th July, 1750.

(f) The seat of government was re-established at Madras on the 5th April, 1752, four years after its restoration to the English by the Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle.

PRESIDENTS AND GOVERNORS OF FORT ST. GEORGE
IN MADRAS—continued.
Name. Assumed charge of office. Remarks.
Lord Pigot (a) ... .. 11 Dec., 1775 Suspended from the service.
George Stratton ... .. 23 Aug., 1776
John Whitehill ... .. 31 Aug., 1777 Acting governor.
Sir Thomas Rumbold, Bart. ... .. 8 Feb., 1778
John Whitehill (b) ... .. 6 April, 1780 Acting governor, 2nd time.
Charles Smith ... .. 8 Nov., 1780 Acting governor.
Lord Macartney, K.B. ... .. 22 June, 1781

(a) Governor for the second time. By order of Mr. George Stratton and the majority of the Council he was placed under arrest and detained at St. Thomas's Mount on the 24th Aug., 1776. He was allowed to return to the Madras Garden House on the 28th April for change of air, and died there on the 10th May, 1777.

(b) Acting governor second time. Suspended by the Governor-General and Council under Section IX of the Regulating Act.

GOVERNORS OF BOMBAY.
Name. Assumed charge of office. Remarks.
Sir Abraham Shipman (a) ... Appointed 'General and Governor' on 19 March, 1662, but was prevented from landing in Bombay by the Portuguese, and died on the island of Anjediva in October, 1664.
Humfrey Cooke ... Secretary to Sir Abraham Shipman, succeeded him in command, and came to Bombay as governor in February, 1665. He remained in power till 5 November, 1666.
Sir Gervase Lucas 5 Nov., 1666 Died 21 May, 1667.
Captain Henry Garey 22 May, 1667 Officiating.
Sir George Oxenden 23 Sept., 1668 Died in Surat, 14 July, 1669.
Gerald Aungier 14 July, 1669 Died in Surat, 30 June, 1677.
Thomas Rolt 30 June, 1677
Sir John Child, Bart. (b) 27 Oct., 1681
Bartholomew Harris 4 Feb., 1690 Died in Surat, 10 May, 1694.
Daniel Annesley 10 May, 1694 Officiating.
Sir John Gayer (c) 17 May, 1694
Sir Nicholas Waite — Nov., 1704
William Aislabie — Sept., 1708
Stephen Strutt 11 Oct., 1715 Officiating.

(a) The first four governors held Bombay for the Crown. The Island was handed over to the Company on the 23rd Sept., 1668. For the next nineteen years (1668–1687), except for occasional visits, and during three years (1672–1673) of Governor Aungier's rule, the Governors of Bombay spent almost the whole of their time in Surat, of which factory they were presidents. During this time, Bombay was administered by an officer styled Deputy-Governor. The transfer, in 1687, of the headquarters of the Company's power to Bombay to a great extent did away with the need of a Deputy-Governor. But in spite of the change the title continued for many years to be borne by the second member of the Council. It would seem to have fallen into disuse some time between 1720 and 1738.

(b) Child was Governor and General, with his headquarters in Bombay, whither he moved from Surat on the 2nd May, 1687, and where he died on the 4th Feb., 1690. In the year 1683, Bombay was the scene of a revolt against the Company's authority. The head of the rebellion was Captain Richard Keigwin, the third member of the Council. Placing the Deputy-Governor under arrest, Keigwin ruled Bombay in the King's name from the 27th Dec., 1683, to the 10th Nov., 1684, when on promise of pardon he handed over the Island to Admiral Sir Thomas Grantham.

(c) Under Gayer, Waite, and Aislabie—that is, from 1694 to 1715—Bombay Governors held the title of General. During the last three years (1701–1704) of his nominal command, Gayer was in confinement in Surat.

(a) Proceeded to Madras on duty in Aug., 1793, and thence joined the Council of the Governor-General as Commander-in-Chief in India on the 28th Oct., 1793.

(b) Was appointed Governor of Bombay by the Honourable the Court of Directors, on the 4th Aug., 1841, but, before he could take charge of his appointment, he was assassinated in Cabul on the 23rd Dec., 1841.

Lieut.-General C. E. Nairne, C.B. ... 4 Sept., 1893 Post abolished on 1 April, 1895.

NOTE.—Mr. J. F. D. Inglis made over charge on the 15th February, 1877, from which date the offices of Chief Commissioner of Oudh and Lieutenant-Governor of the North-Western Provinces were amalgamated under the order of the Government of India, Home Department, No. 45, dated 17th January, 1877 (Gazette of India, 20th January, 1877, Part I).

THE MOST EXALTED ORDER OF THE STAR OF INDIA.

INSTITUTED 1861. ENLARGED 1866, 1875, 1876, 1897, AND 1902.

SOVEREIGN OF THE ORDER.

THE KING, EMPEROR OF INDIA.

GRAND MASTER OF THE ORDER.

The VICEROY and GOVERNOR-GENERAL OF INDIA for the time being
(His Excellency the Right Hon. GILBERT JOHN, EARL OF MINTO, G.C.M.G., G.M.I.E.).

HONORARY KNIGHTS GRAND COMMANDERS (G.C.S.I.).

Mir Sir Muhammad Khudadad Khan, ex-Wali of Kalat, 29 July, 1879.

H.R.H. Sultan Massoud Mirza, Yemin-ed-Dowleh, Zil-es-Sultan, of Persia, 1 Nov., 1887.

C.S.I.

G.C.V.O., C.S.I.

THE MOST EMINENT ORDER OF THE INDIAN EMPIRE.

INSTITUTED 1ST JANUARY, 1878, ENLARGED 1886, 1887, 1892, 1897 AND 1902.

SOVEREIGN OF THE ORDER.

THE KING, EMPEROR OF INDIA.

GRAND MASTER OF THE ORDER.

The VICEROY and GOVERNOR-GENERAL of INDIA for the time being
(His Excellency the Right Hon. GILBERT JOHN, EARL OF MINTO, G.C.M.G., G.M.S.I.).

IMPERIAL ORDER OF THE CROWN OF INDIA.

INSTITUTED 1ST JANUARY, 1878.

SOVEREIGN OF THE ORDER.

THE KING, EMPEROR OF INDIA.

WARRANT OF PRECEDENCE IN INDIA.

VICTORIA, by the Grace of God of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland Queen, Defender of the Faith, Empress of India,—

To all to whom these presents shall come :

WHEREAS it hath been represented unto Us that it is advisable that the rank and precedence of persons holding appointments in the East Indies as regulated by Our Royal Warrant, dated the 18th day of October, 1876, should be altered, We do therefore hereby declare that it is Our will and pleasure that in lieu of the table laid down in Our said recited Warrant, the following table be henceforth observed with respect to the rank and precedence of the persons hereinafter named, viz. :—

  1. 1. Governor-General and Viceroy of India.
  2. 2. Governors of Madras and Bombay.
  3. 3. President of the Council of the Governor-General.
  4. 4. Lieutenant-Governor when in his own territories.
  5. 5. Commander-in-Chief in India.
  6. 6. Lieutenant-Governor.
  7. 7. Chief Justice of Bengal.
  8. 8. Bishop of Calcutta, Metropolitan of India.
  9. 9. Ordinary Members of the Council of the Governor-General.
  10. 10. Commander-in-Chief of Her Majesty's Naval Forces in the East Indies.
  11. 11. Chief Justice of a High Court other than that of Bengal.
  12. 12. Bishops of Madras and Bombay.
  13. 13. Ordinary Members of Council in Madras and Bombay.
  14. 14. Lieutenant-Generals Commanding the Forces, Punjab, Bengal, Madras, and Bombay.
  15. 15. Chief Commissioners of the Central Provinces and Assam, Residents at Hyderabad and in Mysore, and Agents to the Governor-General in Rajputana, Central India, and Baluchistan.
  16. 16. Puisne Judges of a High Court.
  17. 17. Chief Judge of a Chief Court.
  18. 18. Military Officers above the rank of Major-General.
  19. 19. Additional Members of the Council of the Governor-General for making Laws and Regulations.
  20. 20. Bishops of Lahore, Rangoon, Nagpur and Lucknow.
  21. 21. Secretaries to the Government of India, and Joint Secretary to the Government of India in the Public Works Department.
  22. 22. Commissioner in Sind.
  23. 23. Judges of a Chief Court, Recorder of Rangoon and Judicial Commissioners, Burma.
  24. 24. Chief Secretaries to the Governments of Madras and Bombay.
  25. 25. Major-Generals, Members of a Board of Revenue, Commissioners of Revenues and Customs, Bombay, and Financial Commissioners, Punjab and Burma

26. Judicial Commissioners, including Additional Judicial Commissioners of Oudh, the Central Provinces, and Sind.

27. Additional Members of the Councils of the Governors of Madras and Bombay for making Laws and Regulations, Members of the Legislative Council of a Lieutenant-Governor.

28. Vice-Chancellors of Indian Universities.

FIRST CLASS.

29. Members of the Indian Civil Service of 30 years' standing.

30. Advocate-General, Calcutta.

31. Comptroller and Auditor-General.

32. Commissioners of Divisions, the Superintendent of Port Blair, and Residents, Political Agents, and Superintendents drawing Rs. 2,000 a month and upwards (not being Collectors or Deputy Commissioners of British Districts), within their respective charges.

33. Chief Secretaries to Local Governments other than those of Madras and Bombay.

34. Surveyor-General of India, Directors General of the Post Office, of Telegraphs in India, and of Railways, Chief Engineers, first class, the Directors of Railway Construction and Railway Traffic, Accountants-General, Military and Public Works Departments, Director, Royal Indian Marine, and Manager, North-Western Railway.

35. Bishops (not territorial) under license from the Crown.

36. Archdeacons of Calcutta, Madras and Bombay.

37. Brigadiers-General.

38. Commissioners of Divisions.

39. Commissioner of Northern India Salt Revenue, Opium Agents, Benares and Bihar, and Director, Central Criminal Intelligence Department.

40. Secretaries and Joint Secretaries to Local Governments, and Private Secretary to the Viceroy.

SECOND CLASS.

41. Members of the Indian Civil Service of 23 years' standing and Colonels.

42. Military Secretary to the Viceroy.

43. Judicial Commissioners of the Hyderabad Assigned Districts and Baluchistan; the Superintendent of Port Blair; Residents, Political Agents, and Superintendents drawing Rs. 2,000 a month and upwards (not being Collectors or Deputy Commissioners of British Districts).

44. Inspector-General of Forests in India, Director of the Geological Survey, and Director-General of Education in India.

45. Standing Counsel to the Government of India.

46. Directors of Public Instruction, and Inspectors-General of Police and Prisons under Local Governments, and Accountants-General.

47. Survey Commissioner and Director of Land Records and Agriculture, Bombay; Commissioners of Settlements; and Controllers of Military Accounts.

48. Chief or Senior Civil Secretary to a Local Administration.

49. Chief Engineers, second and third classes; Deputy Surveyor-General; Deputy Director-General of Telegraphs in India, and Director-in-Chief, Indo-European Telegraph Department.

50. Divisional, and District and Sessions Judges, Collectors and Magistrates of Districts; Deputy Commissioners of Districts; Deputy Superintendent of Port Blair; and the Chief Officer of each Presidency Municipality; within their respective charges.

51. Archdeacons of Lahore, Lucknow, and Rangoon.

52. Deputy Secretaries to the Government of India.

53. The Senior Chaplains of the Church of Scotland in Bengal, Madras, and Bombay.

54. Remembrancers of Legal Affairs and Government Advocates under Local Governments.

55. Officers in the First Class Graded List of Civil Offices not reserved for Members of the Indian Civil Service.

THIRD CLASS.

56. Members of the Indian Civil Service of 18 years' standing and Lieutenant-Colonels.

57. The Deputy Director, Royal Indian Marine.

58. The Assistant Director, Royal Indian Marine.

59. Commanders and Inspectors of Machinery, Royal Indian Marine.

60. Political Agents and Superintendents drawing less than Rs. 2,000 a month (not being Collectors or Deputy Commissioners of British Districts) within their own charges.

61. Secretaries to Local Administrations other than those already specified.

62. Consulting Engineers to the Government of India for Railways.

63. Private Secretaries to Governors.

64. Military Secretaries to Governors.

65. Administrators-General.

66. Sanitary Commissioners under Local Governments; Postmasters-General; the Comptroller, Post Office; and Conservators of Forests, first grade.

67. Directors of Public Instruction, Inspectors-General of Police and Prisons under Local Administrations, Comptrollers and Deputy Auditors-General, and Deputy Director, Central Criminal Intelligence Department.

68. Managers of State Railways other than the North-Western Railway; Chairman of the Port Trust, Bombay; and Chairman of the Port Trust, Calcutta.

69. Vice-Chairman of the Port Trust, Calcutta; Directors of Traffic and Construction, Indian Telegraph Department; Examiners of Accounts, Public Works Department, first class; Officers of the Superior Revenue Establishment of State Railways, first class, first grade; Superintending Engineers, Public Works Department, first class; Superintendents of the Survey of India Department, first grade.

70. Inspectors-General of Registration and Directors of Land Records and Agriculture, under Local Governments.

71. Senior Chaplains other than those already specified.

72. Sheriffs within their own charges.

73. Officers in the Second Class Graded List of Civil Offices not reserved for Members of the Indian Civil Service.

FOURTH CLASS.

74. Members of the Indian Civil Service of 12 years' standing, and Majors.

75. Lieutenants of over 8 years' standing, and Chief Engineers of the Royal Indian Marine.

76. Government Solicitors.

77. Inspectors-General of Registration, Sanitary Commissioners, and Directors of Land Records and Agriculture under Local Administrations.

78. Officers in the Third Class Graded List of Civil Offices not reserved for Members of the Indian Civil Service.

The entries in the above table apply exclusively to the persons entered therein, and, while regulating their relative precedence with each other, do not apply to the non-official community resident in India, the members of which shall take their place according to usage.

Officers in the above table will take precedence in order of the numbers of the entries. Those included in one number will take precedence inter se according to the date of entry into that number.

When an officer holds more than one position in the table, he will be entitled to the highest position accorded to him.

Officers who are temporarily officiating in any number in the table will rank in that number below permanent incumbents.

All officers not mentioned in the above table, whose rank is regulated by comparison with rank in the army, to have the same rank with reference to civil servants as is enjoyed by Military Officers of equal grades.

All other persons who may not be mentioned in this table, to take rank according to general usage, which is to be explained and determined by the Governor-General in Council in case any question shall arise.

Nothing in the foregoing Rules to disturb the existing practice relating to precedence at Native Courts, or on occasions of intercourse with Natives, and the Governor-General in Council to be empowered to make rules for such occasions in case any dispute shall arise.

All ladies to take place according to the rank herein assigned to their respective husbands, with the exception of wives of Peers, and of ladies having precedence in England independently of their husbands, and who are not in rank below the daughters of Barons, such ladies to take place according to their several ranks, with reference to such precedence in England, immediately after the wives of Members of the Council of the Governor-General.

Given at Our Court at Windsor this tenth day of December, in the year of Our Lord One thousand eight hundred and ninety-eight, and in the sixty-second year of Our Reign.

By Her Majesty's Command.

(Signed) GEORGE HAMILTON.

SUPPLEMENTARY GRADED LIST OF CIVIL OFFICES NOT RESERVED FOR MEMBERS OF THE INDIAN CIVIL SERVICE PREPARED UNDER THE ORDERS OF THE GOVERNOR-GENERAL IN COUNCIL.

*FIRST CLASS—(No. 55 of the Warrant).

Assay Master of the Mint, Calcutta and Bombay.
Chief Judges of Presidency Courts of Small Causes.
Commissioners of Police, Calcutta, Madras, Bombay, and Rangoon.
Deputy Comptroller-General.
Director-General of Archaeology.
Director-General of Statistics.
Director of the Botanical Survey of India.
Inspector-General of Agriculture in India.
Masters of the Mint, Calcutta and Bombay.
Meteorological Reporter to the Government of India.
Superintendent of Revenue Survey, Madras.
Superintendent, Trigonometrical Surveys.

*SECOND CLASS—(No. 73 of the Warrant).

Adviser on Chinese Affairs in Burma.
Agent General in India for the British Protectorates in Africa under the Administration of the Foreign Office.
Chief Collector of Customs, Burma.
Chief Inspector of Mines in India.
Chief Presidency Magistrates.
Chief Superintendents of the Telegraph Department.
Collector of Customs and Salt Revenue, Sind.
Collectors and Magistrates of Districts; and Deputy Commissioners of Districts and of Settlements.
Conservators of Forests, 2nd and 3rd Grades.
Deputy Accountants-General under Local Governments.
Deputy Inspectors-General of Police.
Deputy Superintendent of Port Blair.
Directors of the Persian Gulf Section, and of the Persian Section of the Indo-European Telegraph Department.
Divisional and District and Sessions Judges.
Examiners of Accounts, Public Works Department, 2nd and 3rd Classes.
Government Astronomer, Madras.
Imperial Bacteriologist.
Inspector of Mines to the Government of India.
Librarian, Imperial Library.
Officers in charge of the Records of the Government of India.
Officers of the Indian Educational Service, and of the graded Educational Service drawing Rs. 1,250 a month and upwards.
Officers of the Superior Revenue Establishment of State Railways, 1st Class, 2nd and 3rd Grades.
Principal of the Mayo College at Ajmere.
Principal of the Rajkumar College at Rajkot.
Reporter on Economic Products.
Superintendent of the Royal Botanical Gardens, Calcutta.
Superintendents, Geological Survey of India.

Superintendents of Revenue Survey and Assessment, Bombay.
Superintendents of the Survey of India Department, 2nd Grade.
Superintending Engineers, Public Works Department, 2nd and 3rd Classes.
Under Secretaries to the Government of India.

*THIRD CLASS—(No. 78 of the Warrant).

Agricultural Chemist.
Assistant Inspector-General of Forests.
Assistant Secretaries to the Government of India.
Collector of Stamp Revenue, Superintendent of Excise Revenue, and Deputy Collector of Land Revenue, Calcutta.
Commander of the steamer employed in the Persian Gulf Section of the Indo-European Telegraph Department.
Deputy Collector of Salt Revenue, Bombay.
Deputy Commissioner of Northern India Salt Revenue.
Deputy Commissioners of Police, Calcutta and Bombay.
Deputy Commissioners of Salt, Abkari and Customs Department, Madras.
Deputy Conservators of Forests drawing Rs. 800 a month and upwards.
Deputy Director of Land Records and Agriculture, Madras.
Deputy Director of the Imperial Forest School, Dhera Dun.
Deputy Directors of Revenue Settlements and Deputy Superintendents of Revenue Surveys, Madras.
Deputy Postmasters-General of the 1st, 2nd and 3rd grades.
Deputy Superintendents, Geological Survey of India.
Deputy Superintendents, Survey of India Department.
District Superintendents of Police drawing Rs. 800 a month and upwards.
Engineer and Electrician of the Persian Gulf Section of the Indo-European Telegraph Department.
Examiners of Accounts, Public Works Department, 4th class, 1st and 2nd grades.
Executive Engineers, Public Works Department, 1st and 2nd Grades.
Inspector-General of Railway Mail Service.
Judge of the City Civil Court, Madras.
Judges of Presidency Courts of Small Causes, and First Judge of the Small Cause Court, Rangoon.
Officers of the Indian Educational Service and of the graded Educational Service, drawing less than Rs. 1,250 a month, but more than Rs. 1,000 a month.
Officers of the Superior Revenue Establishments of the State Railways, Second Class, 1st and 2nd Grades.
Palæontologist, Geological Survey of India.
Presidency Magistrates.

* The entries in each class are arranged in alphabetical order.

Protector of Emigrants and Superintendent of Emigration, Calcutta.
Public Prosecutor in Sind.
Registrars to the High Courts and to the Chief Court, Punjab.
Sub-Deputy Opium Agents drawing Rs. 800 a month and upwards.

Superintendent of the Indian Museum.
Superintendent of Land Records and Agriculture in Sind.
Superintendents of Stamps and Stationery.
Superintendents, Telegraph Department, 1st and 2nd Grades.

TABLE OF SALUTES IN INDIA.
Persons. No. of Guns.
Imperial Salute ... .. 101
The King and Emperor when present in person }
Members of the Royal Family ... .. 31 From saluting stations within Indian territories and seas,* and on being fallen in with, by, or visiting any of His Majesty's ships within those limits.
Royal Standard and Royal Salute ... .. 31
Viceroy and Governor-General in India ... .. 31
Independent Asiatic Sovereigns ... .. 21
Other Foreign Sovereigns ... .. 21
Members of their Families and their Standards... .. 21
Ambassadors ... .. 19
Governors of Presidencies ... .. 17
The President of the Council of India ... .. 17
Governor-General of Portuguese Settlements in India 17 From saluting stations within Indian territories and seas, and by His Majesty's ships within those limits.
Governor of Pondicherry ... .. 17
Governors of His Majesty's Colonies ... .. 17
Lieutenant-Governors of Provinces in India ... .. 15
Field-Marshals or Admirals of the Fleet ... .. 17
Commander-in-Chief in India ... .. ... According to military rank, with two guns added.
Commander-in-Chief of His Majesty's Naval Forces ... According to naval rank, with two guns added.
Provisional Commander-in-Chief ... .. ... According to military rank, with two guns added.
Generals and Admirals, or their Flags ... .. 15
Members of Council ... .. 15
Plenipotentiaries and Envoys ... .. 15
Lieut.-Governors of His Majesty's Colonies ... .. 15
Vice-Admirals, Lieut.-Generals, or their Flags ... .. 13
Agents to the Viceroy and Governor-General ... .. 13
Agent to the Governor of Bombay in Kathiawar Residents ... .. 13
Chief Commissioners of Provinces, and Commissioner of Sind 13
Rear-Admirals & Major-Generals, or their Flags 11
Political Agents and Chargés d'Affaires ... .. 11
Commodores of the first-class, and Brigadier-Generals 9
Consuls-General ... .. 9
The Portuguese Governor of Damaun ... .. 9 At any place where there is a saluting station, within the Indian territories and seas, and by His Majesty's ships within those limits.
The Governor of Diu ... .. 9
Return salutes to Foreign Men-of-war ... .. ... Gun for gun, but only when such ships are saluting the national flag on arrival.
Return salutes to Captains of the Navy, and Naval Officers of inferior rank † 7

* Indian territories are understood to include all the waters of India within three miles of the coast thereof. Indian seas to extend from the North-West entrance of the Straits of Malacca to Cape Comorin, excepting Ceylon, and from Cape Comorin to Aden, including the Maldive and Laccadive Islands, and the Persian Gulf.

† Personal salutes are not returned.

ALPHABETICAL LIST, WITH TITLES (ABBREVIATED), OF
NATIVE CHIEFS OF INDIA INCLUDED IN THE PRECEDING
TABLES OF SALUTES.

Ajaigarh. His Highness Maharaja Sawai Sir Ranjor Singh Bahadur, K.C.I.E.
Born 26 Sept., 1849. Succeeded 9 Sept., 1859.

Alirajpur. Rana Partab Singh Bahadur.
Born 1881. Succeeded 14 Feb., 1891.

Alwar. His Highness Maharaja Sawai Jai Singh Bahadur.
Born 14 June, 1882. Succeeded 5 June, 1892.

Bahawalpur. His Highness Nawab Muhammad Bahawal Khan V. Abbasi Bahadur.
Born 23 Oct., 1883. Succeeded 15 Feb., 1899.

Balasinor. Nawab Jamiyat Khanji Bahadur, Babi of.
Born 10 Nov., 1894. Succeeded 11 Sept., 1899.

Bansda. Maharawal Shri Pratapsinghji Gulabsinghji Bahadur, Raja of.
Born 23 Nov., 1864. Succeeded 29 Feb., 1876.

Banswara. His Highness Maharawal Sri Shambhu Singh Bahadur.
Succeeded 30 April, 1905.

Baoni. His Highness Nawab Riaz-ul-Hasan Khan Bahadur.
Born 28 Oct., 1876. Succeeded 2 Aug., 1894.

Baraundha. Raja Thakur Prasad Singh Bahadur.
Born 1847. Succeeded 18 Aug., 1886.

Baria. Maharawal Shri Mansinghji Bahadur, Raja of.
Born 3 Nov., 1855. Succeeded 21 Feb., 1864.

Born 16 Mar., 1863. Succeeded 27 May, 1875.

Born 26 Dec., 1888. Succeeded 14 Dec., 1894.

Born 4 Oct., 1899. Succeeded 27 Aug., 1900.

Born 26 Apr., 1875. Succeeded 10 Feb., 1896.

Bhopal. Her Highness Nawab Sultan Jahan Begum, G.C.I.E.
Born 9 July, 1858. Succeeded 4 July, 1901.

Bijawar. His Highness Maharaja Sawai Sawant Singh Bahadur.
Born 25 Nov., 1877. Succeeded 28 June, 1900.

Born 13 Oct., 1880. Succeeded 19 Aug., 1887.

Bundi. His Highness Maharao Raja Sir Raghubir Singh Bahadur, G.C.I.E., K.C.S.I.
Born 21 Sept., 1869. Succeeded 28 Mar., 1889.

Cambay. His Highness Nawab Jafar Ali Khan Sahib Bahadur.
Born 26 Aug., 1848. Succeeded 11 June, 1880.

Chamba. His Highness Raja Sham Singh Bahadur.
Born 7 July, 1866. Succeeded 17 Apr., 1873.

Charkhari. His Highness Maharaja-Dhiraj Sir Mulkhan Singh Bahadur, K.C.I.E.
Born 25 Oct., 1870. Succeeded 10 July, 1880.

Chhatarpur. His Highness Maharaja Vishwanath Singh Bahadur.
Born 29 Aug., 1866. Succeeded 4 Nov., 1867.

Chota Udaipur. Maharawal Shri Fakhssinghji Bahadur, Raja of.
Born 23 Oct., 1884. Succeeded 8 Feb., 1895.

Cochin. His Highness Raja Sir Rama Varma Bahadur, G.C.S.I.
Born 27 Dec., 1852. Succeeded 23 Oct., 1895.

Cooch Behar. Colonel His Highness Maharaja Sir Nripendra Narayan Bhup Bahadur, G.C.I.E., C.B.
Born 4 Oct., 1862. Succeeded Aug., 1863.

Cutch. His Highness Maharao Sawai Si Khengarji Bahadur, G.C.I.E., Rao of.
Born 23 Aug., 1866. Succeeded 1 Jan., 1876.

Datia. His Highness Maharaja Sir Bhawani Singh Bahadur, K.C.S.I.
Born 13 Aug., 1845. Succeeded 20 Nov., 1857.

Born 1 Jan., 1888. Succeeded 1 Feb., 1900.

Dewas (Junior Branch). His Highness Raja Malhar Rao Bahadur.
Born 10 Aug., 1877. Succeeded 23 May, 1892.

Dhar. His Highness Raja Udaji Rao Bahadur.
